name: Riboflavin (Vitamin B2)
contraindications: No major contraindications at nutritional intakes were highlighted in this source set

sources.title: Riboflavin Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)

description: Riboflavin supports formation of FMN and FAD and is required at low intake levels to prevent deficiency (ariboflavinosis). Migraine studies typically evaluate pharmacologic-dose supplementation rather than dietary-level intake.
summary: Riboflavin (vitamin B2) is an essential vitamin required for FMN/FAD coenzymes in energy and redox metabolism; it has a strong safety profile, while migraine protocols use much higher doses than nutritional intake.
mechanism: Established with proposed extensions: riboflavin supports redox enzymes via FMN/FAD (including antioxidant pathways such as glutathione reductase), while migraine benefit is linked to a proposed mitochondrial-energy mechanism.
safetyNotes: Generally very well tolerated, including at high supplemental doses used in studies.
Bright yellow urine discoloration is common and benign.
No serious toxicity signal is emphasized in the source set.
interactions: No major clinically significant drug interactions are emphasized in the source corpus.
dosage: Migraine protocols commonly use 400 mg/day, which is a pharmacologic dose distinct from dietary-intake targets.
preparation: Available as tablets/capsules and in B-complex products; riboflavin is light sensitive, so protective packaging is common.
regulatoryStatus: Essential vitamin with established dietary reference intakes; marketed as a dietary supplement ingredient.
